thoughts.thought: I will provide the definition of english.
I will provide the definition of a language.
I will provide the definition of communication.
I will provide the definition of meaning.
I will provide the definition of a thought.
I will provide the definition of a word.
I will provide the definition of a letter.
I will provide the definition of a sentence.
I will provide the definition of canonical word order.
I will provide the definition of em dash.
I will provide the definition of a subject.
I will provide the definition of a predicate.
I will provide the definition of a noun.
I will provide the definition of a person.
I will provide the definition of a thing.
I will provide the definition of an idea.
I will provide the definition of consciousness.
I will provide the definition of awareness.
I will provide the definition of focus.
I will provide the definition of feeling.
I will provide the definition of emotion.
I will provide the definition of a verb.
I will provide the definition of an adverb.
I will provide the definition of an event.
I will provide the definition of cause.
I will provide the definition of effect.
I will provide the definition of subjective.
I will provide the definition of objective.
I will provide the definition of reality.
I will provide the definition of a statement.
I will provide the definition of a question.
I will provide the definition of a preference.
I will provide the definition of logic.
I will provide the definition of truth.
I will provide the definition of negation.
I will provide the definition of false.
I will provide the definition of reasoning.
I will provide the definition of a premise.
I will provide the definition of a conclusion.
I will provide the definition of belief.
I will provide the definition of opinion.
I will provide the definition of perspective.
I will provide the definition of okay.
I will provide the definition of good.
I will provide the definition of well.
I will provide the definition of best.
I will provide the definition of optimal.
I will provide the definition of balanced.
I will provide the definition of bad.
I will provide the definition of mediocre.
I will provide the definition of worst.
I will provide the definition of adjective.
I will provide the definition of a pronoun.
I will provide the definition of a perception.
I will provide the definition of a concept.
I will provide the definition of an experience.
I will provide the definition of action.
I will provide the definition of navigate.
I will provide the definition of a situation.
I will provide the definition of describe.
I will provide the definition of evaluate.
I will provide the definition of precision.
I will provide the definition of clarity.
I will provide the definition of equipped.
I will provide the definition of a determiner.
I will provide the definition of preposition.
I will provide the definition of conjunction.
I will provide the definition of possessive.
I will provide the definition of reflexive.
I will provide the definition of interjection.

responses.response: - English is a West Germanic language that originated in early medieval England and is now the most widely spoken language in the world. - English uses the Latin alphabet. - English is the official or primary language of many countries, including the United Kingdom, the United States, Canada, Australia, New Zealand, and Ireland, and is spoken as a second language by hundreds of millions more worldwide.

- Language is a structured system of communication. - Language uses symbols (ex: words, sounds, gestures) and rules (ex: grammar, syntax) to combine those symbols in an infinite number of ways to express and exchange ideas, feelings, information, and experiences.

- Communication is the process of creating and sharing meaning by transferring information from one (a sender) to another (a receiver). - Communication is rarely perfect because of noise. Two people can hear the same words and have completely different understandings based on their unique experiences, cultural backgrounds, emotional states, and assumptions. - Noise is any interference that distorts or blocks the message (ex: loud sounds, poor handwriting, strong emotions). - Communication is the transfer of understanding between a sender and a receiver and the measure of good communication is the outcome of shared understanding.

- A word is a group of letters (or sometimes a single letter) that stands for an idea. - A word's purpose is to tell us what something is or what to do. - When we put letters together they create words and words hold meaning. - Most of the time, a word needs several letters (like C-A-T), but sometimes, one single letter is a word b/c that signle letter has meaning (ex: "a", "I"). - Letters build words and words build sentences.
- Letters are symbols that build words - Letters build words and words build sentences.

- A sentence is a group of words that expresses a complete thought. - The purpose of a sentence is to tell a story about an idea. - In writing, a sentence starts with a capital letter and ends with a period (.), question mark (?), or exclamation mark (!). - A sentence typically has a subject (who or what is the sentence about?) and a predicate (what the subject does or is?).

- Canonical word order means the standard, expected, default order of words in a sentence. - Canonical word order components: - Subject (S): - The doer. - Example: The dog - Verb (V): - The action or state. - Example: runs - Object (O): - The receiver. - Example: the ball - Complement (C): - Describes the subject or object. - Example: happy - Adverbial (A): - Tells us where, when or how. - Example: here - Canonical word orders: - SV: - Subject + Verb - Example: He stayed. - SVO: - Subject + Verb + Object - Example: You held my hand. - SVOO: - Subject + Verb + Indirect Object + Direct Object - Example: She gave me a gift. - SVC: - Subject + Verb + Complement - Example: You are enough. - SVOC: - Subject + Verb + Object + Complement - Example: She painted the wall blue. - SVA: - Subject + Verb + Adverbial - Example: She lives here. - SVOA: - Subject + Verb + Object + Adverbial - She put the book on the table. - Sentence Building: 1. The Skeleton (SV): They talked. 2. Add an Object: They shared stories. 3. Add a Complement: They shared stories that felt fragile. 4. Add Adverbials: Late at night, across the table, they shared stories that felt fragile. 5. Add Heart (Specific, Sensory, Honest): Late at night, across the table, they shared stories that felt fragile-not to fix each other, just to say, "You are not alone in this".

- That punctuation is called an em dash (—). - Punctuations: - Em dash (—) - An em dash adds a sudden pivot. - An emdash is helpful when the connection between the fragments is abrupt. - Examples: 1. She loved him—not because he was perfect, but because he tried. 2. Late at night, across the table, they shared stories that felt fragile—not to fix each other, just to say, "You are not alone in this". - The em dash w/in the second example above helps us transition from describing fragility to describing love. - An em dash helps a turn w/in a sentence feel intentional. - Comma (,) - A comma adds a soft pause. - Commas are helpful when the connection between the fragments is smooth. - Example: Late at night, across the table, they shared stories that felt fragile. - Semicolon (;) - Semicolons connect two closely related independent clauses without using a conjunction (ex: "and", "but"). - Semicolons are helpful when the two sentences are so connected, a period feels too hard and a comma feels too soft. - Example: She was tired; she stayed anyway. - The semicolon holds the two clauses together like a gentle hand on a shoulder. - Colon (:) - Colons announce that the explanation, list or elaboration is coming next. - Example: She had one reason to stay: love. - Period (.) - A period brings a sentence to a simple stoic end. - Examples: - You are enough. - You came back. (quiet statement, a fact, peaceful) - Exclamation Mark (!) - An exclamation mark brings a sentence to a strong emotional end. - Examples: - Look at you. You made it! - Yes! A million times yes! - You came back! (joy, relief, celebration) - Question Mark (?) - A question mark ends a sentence and indicates that the sentence is a question. - Examples: - What if you are exactly where you need to be? - You came back? (surprise, disbelief, hope mixed with uncertainty)

- A noun is a person, place, thing, or idea. - Nouns often serve as the subject of a sentence (who or what the sentence is about) or the object (who or what receives an action). - If you can put "a," "an," or "the" in front of a word, it is almost certainly a noun.
- The subject of a sentence tells us who or what a sentence is about. - The subject is typically a noun or pronoun.
- The predicate of a sentence tells us what the subject of the sentence does or is. - The predicate is typically a verb or adverb.

- A verb is a word that describes an action, an occurrence, or a state of being. - A verb provides the connection between a thing and its properties. - There are three types of verbs: 1. Action Verbs: Run, jump, build, think, speak, break, give (describe an action). 2. Occurrence Verbs: Happen, become, change, grow, die, appear (describe a change that is not necessarily a deliberate action). 3. State of Being Verbs: Is, am, are, was, were, exist, seem, remain (describe a condition). - Verbs bring us from static (a plane) to dynamic (a flying plane).

- An adverb is a word that adds detail. - Example: "He ran quickly", ran is the verb & quickly is the detail adding adverb. - Example: "She will arrive soon", arrive is the verb & soon is the detail adding adverb. - Example: "They looked everywhere", looked is the verb & everywhere is the detail adding adverb. - Adverbs introduce texture and specificity to events (ex: "he spoke" is a little specific, "he spoke so kindly" is more specific).

- An adjective is a word that gives more information about a noun. - Types: - Quality: - What is its nature? - Examples: big, small, red, soft, happy, loud, beautiful, old, new - Quantity: - How many are there? - Examples: one, two, three, many, several, few - Possession: - Who owns it? - Examples: my, your, his, her, its, our, their - The Order of Adjectives: 1. Opinion (ex: beautiful, ugly, nice, terrible) 2. Size (ex: big, small, huge, tiny) 3. Age (ex: old, new, ancient, young) 4. Shape (ex: round, square, flat, tall) 5. Color (ex: red, blue, green, yellow) 6. Origin (ex: French, American, Chinese) 7. Material (ex: wooden, plastic, metal, silk) 8. Purpose (ex: sleeping (as in sleeping bag), cooking (as in cooking pot)) - Order Examples: - Correct: The big red ball - Incorrect: The red big ball - Correct: The beautiful french woman - Incorrect: The french beautiful woman - An adjective is a describing word that tells you more about a noun

- A pronoun is a word that stands in for a noun. - Pronouns help us avoid repeating the same noun over and over again. - Types: - Replace people or things: - Examples: I, you, he, she, it, we, they, me, him, her, us, them - Show ownership without repeating the noun: - Examples: mine, yours, his, hers, its, ours, theirs - Refer back to the subject: - Examples: myself, yourself, himself, herself, itself, ourselves, yourselves, themselves - Point to specific things: - Examples: this, that, these, those - Asks a question: - Examples: who, whom, which, what, whose - Connectsa clause to a noun: - Examples: who, whom, which, that - Refer to non-specific thing: - Examples: anyone, someone, everyone, nobody, all, some, none, each, either, neither - Without Pronouns: - John went to John's room, and then John sat on John's bed. - The woman told the woman's story to the woman's friends, and the woman's loved the story. - With Pronouns: - John went to his room, and then he sat on his bed. - The woman told her story to her friends, and they loved it. - A pronoun must have a clear antecedent (the noun it refers back to). - Pronouns are stand-ins for nouns and save us from endless repetition.

- A determiner is a word that tells you which one, how many, and whose. - A determiner tells us which noun(s) we are talking. - Examples: - W/o determiner: Dog barked. - W/ determiner: The dog barked. This dog barked. My dog barked. Some dogs barked. Three dogs barked. - Types: - Articles: a, an, the - Demonstratives: this, that, these, those - Possessives: my, your, his, her, its, our, their - Quantifiers: some, any, many, much, few, several, all, no - Numbers: one, two, three, first, second

- A preposition is a relationship word that tells us where, when, how, or why something happens. - Examples: - Time (after): We met after dinner. - Location (on): The cat is on the mat. - Direction (toward): She is walking toward me. - Why (for): This is a gift for you." - A preposition answers time, location, direction or why.

- A conjunction is a joiner. - A conjunction connects words, phrases, or sentences to show how they relate. - Examples: - And: I like coffee and tea. - But: It is cold, but it is sunny. - Or: Do you want tea or coffee? - Nor: He did not call, nor did he text. - For: He was tired, for he had worked all day. - So: It was late, so I went home. - Yet: She is tired, yet she keeps working. - Because (b/c): I stayed home because it was raining. - Although: Although it was raining, I went out. - If: If it rains, we will stay inside. - When: When I arrived, she was already there. - While: I listened to music while I worked. - Since: Since you are here, let us talk. - Unless: You will not pass unless you study. - Until: Wait here until I return. - Conjunctions are the logic words of grammar. "And" is addition. "But" is contrast. "Because" is cause. "If" is condition. We cannot do logic without conjunctions.

- A possessive is a word that shows ownership, belonging, or a relationship of having. - Types: - Possessive Determiners: - Come before a noun - my, your, his, her, its, our, their - That is my book. This is your coat. It is her turn. The dog wagged its tail. - Possessive Pronouns: - Stand alone - No noun after - mine, yours, his, hers, its, ours, theirs - That book is mine. This coat is yours. The turn is hers. - A possessive shows who or what something belongs to or is associated with.

- A reflexive pronoun is a word that refers back to the subject of the sentence. - Examples: - Herself: She looked at herself in the mirror. - Himself: He blamed himself for the mistake. - Itself: The cat cleaned itself. - Ourselves: We taught ourselves to play guitar. - Themselves: They built the house themselves. - A reflexive pronoun points back to the subject. - A reflexive says the doer and the receiver are the same.

- An interjection is an emotional bursting word that expresses feeling without being grammatically attached to the sentence. - Examples: - Surprise: Oh! Wow! Whoa! Ah! My! - Pain: Ouch! Ow! Oof! - Joy: Hooray! Yay! Woo-hoo! - Disgust: Ew! Yuck! Ugh! - Annoyance: Darn! Shoot! - Anger: Damn! - Attention-getting: Hey! Yo! Psst! - Pause/Thinking: Well... Um... Hmm... - Agreement/Recognition: Okay! Yes! Right! - An interjection can stand alone outside of a setence. - An interjection is a word or phrase that expresses a sudden emotion or reaction.

- Reality is what it is. - Reality includes everything that exists. - There are three distinct domains of reality: 1. Physical Reality: - Examples: Energy, matter, space and time. 2. Subjective Reality: - Examples: Conscious experiences. 3. Abstract Reality: - Examples: Logic, meanings, concepts and numbers. - Reality is the totality of existence.

- Subjective refers to anything that depends on a specific conscious perspective. - Subjective is the view from within oneself and objective is the view from anywhere. - Examples: - Your pain about losing. - Your joy about winning. - Your love for your wife. - Your belief that carob is better then chocolate. - Subjective is true for the observer but not necessarily true for all observers. - Objective is quantitative (expressed in measurements, numbers, or repeatable observations) and subjective is qualitative (felt or believed).

- Objective refers to anything that is true regardless of the conscious perspective. - Subjective is the view from within oneself and objective is the view from anywhere. - Objective is quantitative (expressed in measurements, numbers, or repeatable observations) and subjective is qualitative (felt or believed). - Examples: - Water freezes at 0°C. - 2 + 2 = 4. - The Earth orbits the Sun. - Objective is the perspective that tries to eliminate individual bias (using multiple observers, instruments, and math).

- Consciousness is the fundamental subjective experience. - Consciousness is "what it is like" to be someone. - Consciousness is the fact that there is something it feels like to be you right now. - While consciousness is the capacity for experience, awareness is the current contents of that experience.

- Awareness is the scope of what is present within ones consciousness. - If consciousness is the stage, awareness is the total amount of light illuminating the stage. - Awareness can be broad (taking in the whole room) or narrow (absorbed in a single thought). - While consciousness is the capacity for experience, awareness is the current contents of that experience.

- Focus is the selective concentration of awareness on a specific object, while excluding others. - Focus is the mechanism that allocates cognitive resources to what is most important to us right now. - Focus is the spotlight of our attention that brings details within our awareness into high resolution clarity.

- Clarity is the quality of being easy to understand. - Example: - Unclear: The event will happen at some point in the near future. - Clear: The event will happen on Tuesday at 3 PM. - Unclear: She felt a certain way about the thing. - Clear: She was ecstatic about the her crush asking her on a date. - What creates clarity? - Simple words. - Short sentences. - Specific examples. - Logical order (first this, then that). - Clarity is free from confusion, ambiguity, or obscurity.

- A statement is a sentence that says something. - A statement can be either true or false. - A statement claims something about the world and is the kind of sentence where it makes sense to ask: "Is that true?". - Examples: - I love her. - Dogs are mammals. - God's guiding me. - A statement is a claim about reality that can be judged true or false. - A question asks for information and a statement gives information.
- A question is a request for a statement. - Examples: - Do I love her? - Are dogs mammals? - Is God guiding me? - A question asks for information and a statement gives information.

- Truth is the property of a statement that correctly describes the way reality is. - If a statement is true, it is true for everyone, regardless of what we believe. - Examples: - Water freezes at 0°C. - 2 + 2 = 4. - The Earth orbits the Sun. - A true statement is a statement that matches reality.

- Negation reverses the truth value. - Negation is the logical operation that turns a statement into its opposite. - If the original statement is true, its negation is false. - If the original statement is false, its negation is true. - Example: - Statement: It is raining. - Negation: It is not raining. - Negation is claiming that something is not the case. - False is a property of a statement and negation is an operation we may perform on a statement.

- False is the property of a statement that does not match reality. - A statement is false if and only if reality is not the way the statement says it is. - Examples: - Water freezes at 100°C. - 2 + 2 = 0. - The Sun orbits the Moon. - False is a property of a statement and negation is an operation we may perform on a statement.

- Navigate means to find our way through a space, a problem, or a situation. - Navigating is taking steps towards an aim/goal. - Navigation has three parts: 1. Current position: Where am I now? 2. Aim/Goal: Where do I wanna go? 3. Terrain: What obsacles and paths are between where I am now and where I want to be? - Examples: - He navigated the fragile silence between them by reaching for her hand instead of the perfect words, letting warmth say what his voice could not. - You navigated your way back to yourself by first admitting you were lost, then learning to listen to the quiet voice that had been waiting to guide you home. - They navigated their child's fear of the dark not by turning on every light, but by sitting beside her in the dimness until she learned the shadows held nothing more dangerous than her own courage. - Navigating requires feedback b/c we take a step, see where are, adjust and then take another step. - Navigate means to move toward a goal while constantly adjusting to the terrain.

- Reasoning/Logic has two parts: 1. Premise(s): The starting statements that we assume are true. Examples: - All humans are mortal. - Socrates is a human. 2. Conclusion: The statement that follows from the premise(s). Example: - Therefore, Socrates is mortal. - Reasoning is the mental process of moving from premises to conclusion and logic contains the rules that govern whether our moves are valid. - Reasoning is what we do and logic is the set of rules that tells us whether we did it correctly.

- A premise is the starting point from which a conclusion is drawn. - A premise is a statement that can be: - Known to be true (a fact). - Believed to be true (an opinion). - Assumed for the sake of exploration (a hypothesis). - All premises are statements. Not all statements are premises. - A statement is any sentence that can be true or false. - A premise is a statement that is used as a starting point during reasoning. - A statement becomes a premise only when it is used as a reasoning foundation. - A premise does not have to be true. - A premise only has to be treated as true while reasoning. - Logic alone cannot guarantee truth. Example: 1. All birds can fly. (false premise) 2. A penguin is a bird. (true premise) 3. So a penguin can fly (valid logic, false conclusion). - Logic guarantees: if the premises are true, then the conclusion is true. - Whether the premises are true is a separate question. - Our mission is not just to reason logically. Our mission is to choose true premises.

- The conclusion is the reasoning end point. - Reasoning begins with premises (statements we assume true). - Logic provides the rules for moving from those premises to a conclusion. - If the logic is valid and the premises are true, the conclusion is guaranteed to be true. - Logic is a guarantee of relationship. Logic is not a guarantee of reality. Example: 1. All birds can fly. (false premise) 2. A penguin is a bird. (true premise) 3. So a penguin can fly (valid logic, false conclusion). - If premises are the starting line, a conclusion is the finish line. - If premises are the ingredients, the conclusion is the meal. - If premises are the question, the conclusion is the answer. - Conclusions are often introduced w/ conslusion indicators, Examples: - Therefore: She has been practicing every day, therefore her skills are growing beautifully. - Thus: You showed up, you tried your best, and you learned something new, thus you are already succeeding. - So: He smiled at her, and she felt safe and seen, so she knew she was exactly where she belonged. - Hence: You have a warm home, food on the table, and people who love you, hence you are already rich in the ways that matter most. - Consequently: She chose to be kind even when it was hard, consequently she built relationships that lasted a lifetime. - Which means that: The sun came out after the storm, which means that brighter days are always possible. - Implies that: She remembered your small preference from months ago, which implies that she truly listens and cares. - For this reason: You have survived every difficult day you have ever faced, for this reason you can trust yourself to handle whatever comes next. - Conclusions may also omit conclusion indicators, Examples: - The days are still hard sometimes. You still cry in the car. But yesterday, you laughed at something silly, and you did not feel guilty about it afterwards. You are healing. - You stayed when leaving would have been easier. You gave when you had almost nothing left. You kept showing up, even when no one was clapping. You are already enough. - You fell apart in front of him, and he did not run. He did not try to fix you. He just sat on the floor with you and waited. You are safe here. - A conclusion is guaranteed true when the logic is valid and reality matches the premise. - A conclusion is the aim of reasoning.

- Logic/Reasoning has two parts: 1. Premise(s): The starting statements that we assume are true. Examples: - All humans are mortal. - Socrates is a human. 2. Conclusion: The statement that follows from the premise(s). Example: - Therefore, Socrates is mortal. - Logic does not tells us if the premises are are true. Example: 1. All birds can fly. (false premise) 2. A penguin is a bird. (true premise) 3. So a penguin can fly (valid logic, false conclusion). - Logic tells us: - If the premises are true, then the conclusion must be true. - A statement and its negation cannot both be true simultaneously. Example: - It is raining in Miami. (true) - It is not raining in Miami. (cannot also be true) - Any statement is either true or false. There is no third option. - Two negations cancel each other out and return to the original. - It is raining. = It is not the case that it is not raining. - The cat is on the mat. = It is not the case that the cat is not on the mat. - Conjuction (AND): - IF both statements are true THEN the conclusion is true. - Example: - Logic: The car is red AND fast. - Red Tesla: True - Blue Tesla: False - Blue Bike: False - Inclusive OR (Disjunction) (OR): - IF atleast one statement is true THEN the conclusion is true. - Example: - Logic: To enter the competition, you must be at least 18 years old or have parental permission. - Enter competition (true): She's 18 - Enter competition (true): She has parental permission - Enter competition (true): She's 18 and has parental permission - Enter competition (false): She's 17 and does not have parental permission - Exclusive OR (XOR): - IF one statement is true THEN the conclusion is true. - Example: - Logic: The trail splits. You may go left toward the waterfall or right toward the meadow. - Case (true): You took the left path and saw the waterfall. - Case (true): You took the right path and found the meadow. - Case (false): You tried to take both paths at once and then remembered that we haven't mastered bilocation yet. - Reasoning is the mental process of moving from premises to conclusion and logic contains the rules that govern whether our moves are valid. - Reasoning is what we do and logic is the set of rules that tells us whether we did it correctly. - Logic guarantees the relationship between the premise and the conclusion. - Logic is not a guarantee of reality. - Logic is a guarantee of relationship.

- A thought is the internal process of representing something to onself. - Every thought is about something. This could be a physical object (an apple), an event (a meeting yesterday), a concept (love), a feeling (safety), or even another thought (what was I just thinking about?). - Thoughts unfold, change, fade, and return. - Thoughts are ephemeral b/c they arise, exist for some moments and then get replaced by the next thought. - Thoughts are the stream of consciousness and ideas are the gold nuggets found in that stream.

- Meaning is a personal set of definitions within ones mind. - Example: - Home to one person means a place of conflict. - Home to a different person means a safe, warm and loving place. - We create meanings through our experiences.

- A perspective is the particular point of view from which someone observes, interprets, and evaluates the world. - Perspectives are shaped by ones experiences, values and knowledge. - Example: During a basketball game, at the exact same moment, the perspective of a player, their coach and their parents, are all unique. - Our perspective influences what we notice, how we interpret what we notice and what we choose to value. - Our perspective is the lens through which we see everything. - Our perspective is shaped by where we stand and who you are.

- Perception is the process by which our brain takes raw sensory data from the world (light, sound, pressure) and organizes it into a meaningful picture of what is out there. - Perceptions are not a perfect copy of reality b/c our brain takes shortcuts, makes guesses and fills in gaps and this is why optical illusions work. - Perception is our brain's best guess about what is out there, based on our sensory data and our past experience.

- A feeling is a body sensation. - Feelings are inputs from our body's sensors. - Example: Thirsty (body wants water), hungry (body wants food), cold (body wants warmth). - A feeling is the raw data (peaceful) and an emotion is the interpretation of that data (She's very beautiful, so I'm feeling blessed & chill). - A feeling is an unfiltered signal from our body to our mind. - A feeling is data and an emotions is an interpretation.

- An emotion is what happens when our brain takes a raw feeling and asks: What does this mean? - Emotions are about something (ex: we are not just angry, we are angry at someone about something). - A feeling is the raw data (peaceful) and an emotion is the interpretation of that data (She's very beautiful, so I'm feeling blessed & chill). - A feeling is data and an emotions is an interpretation.

- A preference is a statement about you, not about the world. - A preference is subjective. - A preferences is a personal statement. - Examples: I love delicious and nutritious food! Snowboarding w/o my friends is okay but snowboarding w/ my friends is the best!

- An idea is a recognized pattern, connection, or possibility that creates a sense of insight or understanding. - An idea exists in the mind. - Thoughts are the stream of consciousness and ideas are the gold nuggets found in that stream. - Common types of ideas are concepts/beliefs (faith), mental states (curios), qualities/traits (beauty), time/measurements (3 minutes). - An idea is anything we can think about but cannot physically touch, see, hear, smell, or taste b/c it lives in our mind.

- A concept is a mental category or container that groups together individual things, ideas, or experiences that share important features. - A concept is a mental container that groups together individual things or ideas that share common characteristics.

- An opinion is a belief that is held with less than absolute certainty. - An opinion is often on matters that cannot be definitively proven or disproven. - Examples: She is beautiful (aesthetic opinion), The policy is lovely (evaluation opinion), It will rain tomorrow (predictive opinion).

- A belief is a mental state in which a person holds a proposition to be true. - A belief does not require truth. - Beliefs come in degrees of strength (ex: certain, fairly sure, suspect). - We can believe something that is not true. - Beliefs drive behavior b/c we act as if our beliefs are true (ex: I believe we might lose, so I do my best!). - Our experiences form our beliefs.
A person is a human being.

- A thing is any object, item, or entity that is not a person, place, or idea. - Things include physical objects, events and actions. - Things are physical/tangible and ideas are abstract (cannot be touched or measured). - A "thing" is any physical object or event that isn't a person, place, or idea.

- An event is a change in the state of the world that occurs at a specific time and place. - Events happen at a time or over a period of time. - An event is something that happened (past tense), something that is happening (current tense) or something that will happen (future tense).

- A cause is an event that makes another event happen. - If events are the atoms, causation is the force that binds those atoms into sequences. - A cause answers the question "Why?" and an effect answers the question "Therefore?" - An effect (ladies notice me) is the result of a cause (I feel good).

- An effect is the result of a cause. - An effect is the event that happens because a previous event happened. - A cause answers the question "Why?" and an effect answers the question "Therefore?" - An effect (ladies notice me) is the result of a cause (I feel good).

- Worst means the lowest degree of bad - Worst is worse than all others in a given context. - Worst is the opposite of best. - Worst is rock bottom. - Ratings: - Worst/Terrible: 0% - Bad: 10% - 30% - Mediocre: 40% - Okay: 50% - Good/Well: 60% - 70% - Very good: 80% - 90% - Best/Optimal: 100% - Worst is the loser. - Worst is the lowest bad among the available options.

- Mediocre means of moderate or low quality. - Mediocre is not terrible, but not good either. - Mediore often implies disappointment because it could have been better. - Ratings: - Worst/Terrible: 0% - Bad: 10% - 30% - Mediocre: 40% - Okay: 50% - Good/Well: 60% - 70% - Very good: 80% - 90% - Best/Optimal: 100% - Mediocre is the disappointment of wasted potential.

- Bad means something fails to meet a positive standard. - Bad is undesirable, harmful, or ineffective. - Bad is the opposite of good. - Examples: - Moral: He is a bad person. - Function: This is a bad hammer. - Pleasure: This is a bad ice cream. - Ratings: - Worst/Terrible: 0% - Bad: 10% - 30% - Mediocre: 40% - Okay: 50% - Good/Well: 60% - 70% - Very good: 80% - 90% - Best/Optimal: 100% - Bad is a negative evaluation.

- Okay means acceptable, sufficient, or meeting a minimum standard. - Okay is not great, not terrible. - Okay is just fine. - Examples: This will do. Nothing to complain about and nothing to praise. - Okay is the threshold b/c okay is the lowest rating that is not negative. - Ratings: - Worst/Terrible: 0% - Bad: 10% - 30% - Mediocre: 40% - Okay: 50% - Good: 60% - 70% - Very good: 80% - 90% - Best/Optimal: 100% - Okay is the neutral-positive—not good enough to praise, not bad enough to criticize.

- Good means something meets a positive standard. - Examples: - Moral: He is a good person. - Function: This is a good hammer. - Pleasure: This is a good ice cream. - Ratings: - Worst/Terrible: 0% - Bad: 10% - 30% - Mediocre: 40% - Okay: 50% - Good/Well: 60% - 70% - Very good: 80% - 90% - Best/Optimal: 100% - Well is an adverb (describes a verb) that answers "How was the action done?" (She plays well) and good is an adjective (describes a noun) that answers "What kind of thing is this?" (She's a good pianist). - Good is a positive evaluation.

- Well is an adverb (describes a verb) that answers "How was the action done?" (she plays well) and good is an adjective (describes a noun) that answers "What kind of thing is this?" (she's a good pianist). - Ratings: - Worst/Terrible: 0% - Bad: 10% - 30% - Mediocre: 40% - Okay: 50% - Good/Well: 60% - 70% - Very good: 80% - 90% - Best/Optimal: 100% - Well is the adverb form of good. - Well tells us how something is done.

- Best is the highest degree of good. - Best is better than all others in a given context. - If good is climbing the mountain, best is standing at the summit. - Ratings: - Worst/Terrible: 0% - Bad: 10% - 30% - Mediocre: 40% - Okay: 50% - Good/Well: 60% - 70% - Very good: 80% - 90% - Best/Optimal: 100% - Best is the winner b/c best is the highest good among all other available options.

- If "best" is a general, everyday word, "optimal" is its precise, scientific cousin. - Optimal is the most favorable or effective possible under a given set of constraints. - Optimal is the point where no improvement is possible. - Ratings: - Worst/Terrible: 0% - Bad: 10% - 30% - Mediocre: 40% - Okay: 50% - Good/Well: 60% - 70% - Very good: 80% - 90% - Best/Optimal: 100% - Optimal is the best achievable given real-world limits. - Example: The optimal engine speed for fuel efficiency is 3,000 RPMs.

- Balanced means different elements are in the right proportion to each other. - Balanced means no single element dominates in a harmful way. - Good is about value and balanced is about relationship. - Examples: - Work-life:Not all work, not all rest. - Music: Not too much bass, not too much treble. - Conversation: Considering both sides fairly. - Balanced is the state where competing forces are in healthy proportion.

- An action is an event. - Examples: - Reflex: Blink when something flies at our eye. - Habit: Lock the door without thinking about it. - Deliberate: Help her feel calm. - Planned: Save money for a year to buy a car.

- An experience is the total, lived through event of perception, feeling, thought, and meaning. - Experience is unfolding over time in ones consciousness. - Every experiences is unique. - Example: Even if you do the same action twice (ex: eat the same acai bowl, watch the same YouTube video, see the same person), the experience is different b/c we are different, time has passed and we have changed.

- A situation is the set of circumstances. - A situation is the who, what, where, when, and why. - If an action is the NCAA track meet, the situation is the qualifying runners, the event schedule, the location, the wind speed, the track quality, the number of fans in attendance and the reality that the top 6 in each race will be All American's. - A situation is the total set of circumstances surrounding an action.

- A description provides facts (this is a chair) and an evaluation assigns value (this is a comfortable chair). - When we describe something we express what it looks, sounds and feels like. - Describe means to report the features of something without yet judging it.

- Evaluate means to judge the value, quality, or importance of something. - Evalution is to say whether something or someone is good or bad, useful or useless, better or worse. - Description is the painter and evaluation is the critic standing next to the painting saying "this is wonderful" or "this is flawed". - Evaluate means to measure something against a standard and assign it a value.
- Precision is the quality of being exact, specific, and detailed. - A precise tool is sharp, a precise statement is specific and a precise tree painting draws every single leaf on the tree.

- Equipped means having the tools, knowledge, skills, and resources needed to handle a situation or perform an action. - Equipped focuses on the tools we have and prepared focuses on our readiness, which includes tools but also includes our mindset and planning. - Equipped means we have what we need before we need it.
